questions.question: Explain the difference between General Purpose Registers and Special Function Registers (SFRs) in 8051.
keyPoints: General purpose registers (R0-R7) are in internal RAM, used for temporary data storage
SFRs have predefined functions (ports, timers, interrupts) mapped to 80H-FFH
SFRs control hardware features like I/O, serial communication, and timers
GPRs can be accessed directly; SFRs can be accessed by name or address

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Program Status Word (PSW) register? List all flag bits with their positions.
keyPoints: PSW is an 8-bit register showing status of arithmetic operations
Bit 7: CY (Carry flag)
Bit 6: AC (Auxiliary Carry)
Bit 5: F0 (User-defined flag)
Bit 4-3: RS1, RS0 (Register bank select)
Bit 2: OV (Overflow flag)
Bit 1: Reserved
Bit 0: P (Parity flag)

questions.question: How is the register bank selection done in 8051? Show the bit combinations for selecting each bank.
keyPoints: RS1 and RS0 bits in PSW select the active register bank
Bank 0: RS1=0, RS1=0 (addresses 00H-07H)
Bank 1: RS1=0, RS1=1 (addresses 08H-0FH)
Bank 2: RS1=1, RS0=0 (addresses 10H-17H)
Bank 3: RS1=1, RS0=1 (addresses 18H-1FH)
Default is Bank 0 after reset

questions.question: Explain the functions of Program Counter (PC) and Stack Pointer (SP) registers.
keyPoints: PC is a 16-bit register that holds address of next instruction to execute
PC automatically increments after each instruction fetch
SP is an 8-bit register pointing to top of stack in internal RAM
SP initialized to 07H after reset, incremented before PUSH, decremented after POP
Used for CALL, RET, PUSH, POP instructions

questions.question: What is the Data Pointer (DPTR) register? Explain its structure and applications.
keyPoints: DPTR is a 16-bit register used as a data pointer
Consists of two 8-bit registers: DPH (high byte) and DPL (low byte)
Used to access external data memory with MOVX instruction
Also used with MOVC to access code memory look-up tables
Can be incremented/decremented using INC DPTR

questions.question: Describe the internal RAM organization of 8051. Explain the different memory areas and their purposes.
keyPoints: Internal RAM is 128 bytes (00H-7FH)
Lower 32 bytes (00H-1FH): 4 register banks (R0-R7 each)
Next 16 bytes (20H-2FH): Bit-addressable area (128 bits)
Upper 80 bytes (30H-7FH): General purpose RAM
SFRs occupy 80H-FFH (128 bytes)
Register banks selectable via PSW RS1,RS0 bits
Bit-addressable area allows individual bit manipulation

questions.question: Explain the accumulator architecture in 8051. How is it used in arithmetic operations? Discuss the B register's role.
keyPoints: Accumulator (A) is primary register for ALU operations (8-bit)
Most arithmetic/logical operations use accumulator as one operand
B register works with A for multiplication and division
MUL AB: A × B, result in B (high) and A (low)
DIV AB: A ÷ B, quotient in A, remainder in B
A holds results of ADD, SUB, AND, OR, etc.
Parity bit in PSW reflects number of 1s in A

questions.question: Explain the interrupt-related Special Function Registers in 8051. Discuss IE and IP registers with bit definitions.
keyPoints: IE (Interrupt Enable) at A8H: EA(7), -(6), ES(5), ET1(4), EX1(3), ET0(2), EX1(1), EX0(0)
EA enables all interrupts when 1, individual bits enable specific interrupts
IP (Interrupt Priority) at B8H: -(7), -(6), PS(5), PT1(4), PX1(3), PT0(2), PX1(1), PX0(0)
Priority bits: 1=high priority, 0=low priority
5 interrupt sources: IE0, TF0, IE1, TF1, Serial
Default priority: IE0>TF0>IE1>TF1>Serial

questions.question: Explain the timer-related SFRs in 8051. Discuss TMOD, TCON, TH, and TL registers and their functions.
keyPoints: TMOD (Timer Mode): Gate, C/T, M1, M0 for Timer 1 and Timer 0
TCON (Timer Control): TF1, TR1, TF0, TR0, IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0
TH (Timer High) and TL (Timer Low) form 16-bit timer register
Mode 0: 13-bit timer, Mode 1: 16-bit timer
Mode 2: 8-bit auto-reload, Mode 3: Split timer mode
TR bit starts/stops timer, TF indicates overflow
